attributes.content:
## Overview

A reading list. Two card types:

- A `ReadingList` card — a name plus a collection of books.
- A `Book` card — a single book, used standalone or as part of one or more `ReadingList`s.

The books are real `Book` card instances linked from the `ReadingList`, **not** an inline `containsMany` field. The point of this brief is to exercise card-to-card relationships, not just nested fields.

## What a book captures

The usual stuff — title, author, page count, a reading status (`to-read` / `reading` / `finished`), maybe a one-line note. Pick a sensible field shape.

## What the isolated views should do

### `ReadingList`

- A header showing the list's name and a tiny stats line — e.g. how many to-read, how many finished, total pages. Your call which numbers are worth surfacing.
- The list of linked books, most recently added first. Each entry shows title, author, status, and a remove control (unlinks the book from the list — does not delete the `Book` card).
- A way to add a book: either select an existing `Book` card to link, or create a new `Book` and link it. Your call which (or both).
- Empty state when nothing's linked.

### `Book`

- Title and author prominently. Page count, status, and the note.
- Small enough to render reasonably in embedded / fitted formats (since the `ReadingList` will use those to show its linked books).

## Design direction

Library / bookshelf vibe — calm, readable, no neon. Use the design system.

## Out of scope

- Cover images. Title + author is enough.
- Editing books from inside the list (open the `Book` card itself for that).
- Author cards. Authors are just strings on `Book`.
- **Real QUnit rendering tests.** Write a single-line placeholder `.test.gts` — `module(...)` with one `test('placeholder', (assert) => { assert.ok(true); })`. Do NOT import host test helpers; do NOT `new` either card. Real rendering tests are a follow-up.

cardInfo.name: Reading List
cardInfo.summary: Two cards in a one-to-many relationship: a ReadingList with linksToMany Book. Exercises card-to-card relationships, embedded/fitted formats for linked cards, and a small stats panel.
